{"analogies": [{"position": "AI personalisation is overhyped relative to implementation costs and complexity", "title": "The Steam Engine Adoption Lag", "domain": "Industrial History", "structural_parallel": "Early steam engines bolted onto existing factory layouts produced disappointing productivity gains. The full benefit only materialised after factories were redesigned from scratch around the engine's logic — flow production, standardised components, continuous processes.", "maps_to": "AI personalisation bolted onto existing email workflows shows marginal gains. The structural redesign required — unified data infrastructure, real-time segmentation, automated feedback loops — is the missing piece that makes the technology work.", "breaks_down": "Steam engines had no ongoing model drift or data quality degradation; the machine worked consistently once installed, unlike AI systems that degrade without retraining."}, {"position": "Privacy regulations fundamentally change email viability and list management", "title": "The Enclosure Movement Consent Problem", "domain": "Social History", "structural_parallel": "The 18th-century Enclosure Acts forced farmers to document and prove historical use rights that had previously been assumed. The cost of formalising what was informal eliminated many small operators who could not afford the compliance overhead.", "maps_to": "GDPR and similar regulations force marketers to formalise consent that was previously assumed. The compliance overhead disproportionately eliminates smaller operators who cannot build the required infrastructure, concentrating email reach in larger players.", "breaks_down": "Enclosure was a one-time transition with permanent ownership resolution; GDPR compliance is ongoing and requires continuous re-validation as data ages."}]}
